EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused some minor flash flooding in Daviess County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported water over several roads in Gallatin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With slick roads causing accidents across the Kansas City Metro one such accident proved fatal, just on the Missouri side of the Missouri River along I-635 near where it intersects with Horizons Parkway. Icy roads have not been determined officially as the cause, but with icy roads that day it's strongly plausible as a contributor to the accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An accident along I-635 just north of the Missouri River was fatal for one motorist. Icy roads have not been determined officially as the cause, but with 2 to 4 inches of snow on the roads causing slick conditions that morning it's strongly plausible as a contributor to the accident. A vehicle was travelling south along I-635 and crashed head on into a semi. This took place south of Horizons Parkway along I-635, but north of the Missouri River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable airmass in place across the Mid-South helped to fuel severe storms ahead of an advancing cold front and deep upper-level trough swinging into the Central Plains. A line of severe storms moved from Arkansas across northern parts of the Mid-South during the early morning hours of May 4th. There was widespread wind damage and a few weak tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were knocked down in Bragg City.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado developed near Interstate 55 where it damaged a shop building and a grain bin. Debris from the shop building was carried approximately one-quarter mile downstream. On the afternoon of October 24, 2021, several tornadoes formed across northern Missouri. These tornadoes produced some isolated damage, but mostly remained over open territory. The most impactful tornado formed near Chillicothe, and went on to produce EF-2 damage in Purdin, Missouri. There were no reported injuries from these tornadoes despite the visible heavy damage in certain locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of the tornado that came out of Livingston County. The tornado continued across the entirety of Linn County. For the most part the tornado impacted mainly rural areas of the county, but the tornado did brush by the south side of Purdin, Missouri, causing EF-2 damage to residences near Highway 5. The tornado was well reported by chasers as it approached the northeast corner of Linn County. The tornado was reported dissipated as the storm crossed into Macon and Adair Counties. This was corroborated on the survey with the last damage point in the far northeast corner of Linn County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado developed just east of Interstate 35 in Clinton County, MO. It quickly crossed into Caldwell County, MO where it damaged several trees and a few buildings. The tornado dissipated just east of Goose Creek and State Route D.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure center tracked northeast through southeast Missouri into central Illinois during the afternoon and evening. The track of the low resulted in increasing low level winds in the Mississippi River counties of southeast Missouri in the evening. West to southwest winds gusted to between 40 and 45 mph at times. The peak wind gust was measured at 45 mph at the Cape Girardeau airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large three limbs were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of storms, followed by a bowing line of thunderstorms, organized and accelerated east-northeastward during the overnight and wee morning hours. Several pockets of damaging winds accompanied the storms. In addition, a mesovortex along the bowing line of storms produced a tornado near the Mississippi River. A mid-level shortwave trough moved east across the southern Plains. A belt of stronger southwesterly 500 mb winds spread northeast ahead of the trough into southeast Missouri and Arkansas. Instability was sufficient for a round of severe thunderstorms to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported trees down on roads in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed from southwest Missouri into central Illinois late in the morning. These storms then moved east-southeastward through the afternoon, producing isolated strong winds in southeast Missouri. The storms occurred south and east of a cold front trailing from a low in southwestern Iowa back across Kansas to the Texas Panhandle. Some daytime heating and destabilization resulted in a gradual increase in coverage and intensity of storms. Wind shear was sufficient for organization of the storms. Around 30 knots of low-level south-southwesterly flow veered to west-southwesterly at mid levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A medium-size live tree was blown down across a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms strengthened along and ahead of a cold front moving southeastward into southeast Missouri. As daytime heating continued through the afternoon, the storms produced isolated hail up to nickel size. Cold mid-level temperatures and resulting steep lapse rates supported moderate instability, despite relatively modest low-level moisture.

The fog formed under a narrow ridge of high pressure that extended up and down the Mississippi River Valley. The fog formed under clear skies and light winds following a day of rain.

The peak measured wind gust at the Cape Girardeau and Poplar Bluff airports was 43 mph. A rather strong low pressure center over the central Plains contributed to the gusty south winds.

A storm system that moved from the southern High Plains into the Mid-Mississippi Valley brought widespread rain and strong to severe thunderstorms to locations in Missouri near the Kansas, Oklahoma and Arkansas borders during the afternoon and evening. Sporadic reports of hail larger than quarters and wind damage were received from these locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Howell-Oregon electric cooperative reported that numerous power outages numbering in the hundreds occurred due to high winds. Several public weather stations in the area measured wind gusts 45 to 55 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced isolated large hail up to the size of golf balls, along with isolated wind damage during the afternoon and evening. Between 3:30 and 7:30 PM, approximately 20 reports of hail dime-size or larger hail or wind damage were received. The vast majority of reports were large hail. Semi-discrete convection formed within a broad confluence zone. Surface temperatures exceeded 80 degrees, with surface dewpoints barely reaching 50. A dry boundary layer supported low-level lapse rates exceeding 9 C/km across much of the Ohio Valley. Up to 1000 J/kg of mixed-layer cape and 30-40 kts of effective bulk wind shear supported large hail with the strongest, longest lived updrafts. The dry boundary layer also supported efficient downward momentum transport of some of the stronger downdraft cores.

Heavy rain developed over southern Missouri on the night of the 27th and continued through the evening of the 28th as a slow moving cold front moved across the region and interacted with a moist and unstable airmass over the region. Several inches of rain fell in a short period of time during the early morning of the 28th and led to flash flooding in Reeds Spring and at Roaring River State Park, where numerous campers were evacuated. Between 1.50 and 5 inches of rain fell across much of the Ozarks in a 24 hour period. Some readings indicated that as much as 2.5 inches of rain fell in about a 30 minute period In Barry and Stone Counties. In addition to the flooding, several brief tornadoes occurred in Barry, Christian and Douglas Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey concluded that three large outbuildings were slightly to heavily damaged from high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three consecutive nights of frost or freezing temperatures were observed from the 1st to the 3rd. Vegetation was vulnerable to frost and freezing temperatures due to abnormally warm temperatures in March that promoted early growth. The coldest temperatures were on the morning of the 2nd, when lows were in the 20's. The exception was the Bootheel area from Sikeston south and east, where lows were in the lower 30's. Minor to moderate crop damage was reported after a full assessment was conducted. Alfalfa and wheat sustained light damage in many areas. More than half the peach and apple crop sustained no damage, however a significant percentage of both crops were damaged to varying degrees, ranging from light to severe. Protected strawberries were undamaged, and the overall condition of the crop was rated as good in early May. An unseasonably strong high pressure system with a central pressure around 1035 mb moved southeast from Canada across the lower Ohio Valley on the morning of the 2nd.

Up to 1000 J/kg of mixed-layer cape and 30-40 kts of effective bulk wind shear supported large hail with the strongest, longest lived updrafts. The dry boundary layer also supported efficient downward momentum transport of some of the stronger downdraft cores.

A late-season blast of cold air brought sub-freezing temperatures to most areas on the night of the 20th. Lows ranged from 27 to 32 degrees, except for some 33-degree readings near the Kentucky border in the New Madrid and Charleston areas. Cape Girardeau set a record low of 29 degrees on the 21st, and Poplar Bluff tied a record low of 31. This was tied for the fourth latest freeze on record at Poplar Bluff. There were no immediate reports of crop damage due to the cold air. A strong cold front moved southeast across the region on the evening of the 19th, producing some light snow in some areas. Fruitland in Cape Girardeau County measured an inch on grassy surfaces. A low pressure system then moved northeast along the front, passing over the Tennessee Valley on the 20th. Gusty north winds on the back side of the low ushered in the cold air.

Minor flooding on the Mississippi River continued from March into the first week of April. An active spring-like pattern in March caused river flooding. There were a couple rounds of heavy rain, somewhere regionally within the Mississippi basin, that contributed to the river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding ended on the Mississippi River on the 2nd of April. Some low-lying fields were underwater.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding on the Mississippi River caused some low-lying fields and woodlands to go underwater.

The remnants of a squall line moved east across southeast Missouri in the form of a loosely organized cluster of storms. These storms moved along a poorly defined warm front, characterized by weak instability and 40 to 50 kt of effective bulk wind shear. There was sufficient low level wind shear for an isolated tornado. The Paducah, KY radar wind profile indicated 0-3 km storm-relative helicity was just over 150. As the storms progressed through this environment, they produced a couple damaging gusts and a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding of low-lying streets was reported in Poplar Bluff. Rainfall of 1.2 inches was measured in 30 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-level high pressure re-strengthened over the Mid-South in late August resulting in hot and humid conditions. Afternoon heat index values greater than 105 degrees were widespread across the Mid-South.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index values reached 105 to 110 degrees in the afternoon and early evening.

Southerly flow as well as intermittent showers and a few thunderstorms raised the dew point into the mid 60s to around 70 across the area.  Also, forecast soundings showed a very moist and deep warm layer up to around 12,000 feet.  This combined with strong 850mb moisture convergence across southern Missouri and Illinois and precipitable water values in the 99th plus percentile, set the region up for a heavy rain and flash flood event. Up to three inches of rain fell in a short amount of time causing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to three inches of rain fell in a short amount of time onto already saturated soil. This caused flash flooding. In Cuba, the viaduct near the railroad tracks flooded. Also, portions of Highway 19 and Route PP were flooded. Sections of Highway N southeast of Bourbon were impassable. A number of low water crossings and roads in the Leasburg area were flooded and the water was several feet deep in these locations.

Also, forecast soundings showed a very moist and deep warm layer up to around 12,000 feet.  This combined with strong 850mb moisture convergence across southern Missouri and Illinois and precipitable water values in the 99th plus percentile, set the region up for a heavy rain and flash flood event. Up to three inches of rain fell in a short amount of time causing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to three inches of rain fell in a short amount of time onto already saturated soil. This caused flash flooding. Many small creeks were flooded across northern portions of the county. Also, several low water crossings and roads had several feet of water over them, especially in the Richwoods area.

In Fletcher, Calico Creek rose well out of its banks flooding Calico Creek Road, Old Highway H and Nickelson Road.  The intersection of Highways Y and C in front of Grandview High School was flooded. A number of roads in the De Soto area had several feet of water over them.  Larry G. Crites Memorial Park, northwest of Festus, was flooded due to Joachim Creek.

These storms moved along a poorly defined warm front, characterized by weak instability and 40 to 50 kt of effective bulk wind shear. There was sufficient low level wind shear for an isolated tornado. The Paducah, KY radar wind profile indicated 0-3 km storm-relative helicity was just over 150. As the storms progressed through this environment, they produced a couple damaging gusts and a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage was mainly to trees, with thousands of large trees snapped or uprooted. Near the beginning of the path, along Highway M, south of Patton, a tractor trailer was overturned. A smaller trailer and several farm items were blown around, such as a fuel tank and planks. Extensive tree damage began by the time the tornado reached County Road 346, with thousands of trees downed between County Road 346 and Missouri JJ Highway. A small shed was blown away just south of JJ Highway, where the tornado reached its maximum intensity and width. A mobile home lost some of its shingles and most of its underpinning as the tornado moved along and just north of JJ Highway. The tornado ended just northeast of the intersection of JJ Highway and County Road 366, where it downed several mainly small trees and removed some shingles from a garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system that moved from the southern High Plains into the Mid-Mississippi Valley brought widespread rain and strong to severe thunderstorms to locations in Missouri near the Kansas, Oklahoma and Arkansas borders during the afternoon and evening. Sporadic reports of hail larger than quarters and wind damage were received from these locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickle sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain developed over southern Missouri on the night of the 27th and continued through the evening of the 28th as a slow moving cold front moved across the region and interacted with a moist and unstable airmass over the region. Several inches of rain fell in a short period of time during the early morning of the 28th and led to flash flooding in Reeds Spring and at Roaring River State Park, where numerous campers were evacuated. Between 1.50 and 5 inches of rain fell across much of the Ozarks in a 24 hour period. Some readings indicated that as much as 2.5 inches of rain fell in about a 30 minute period In Barry and Stone Counties. In addition to the flooding, several brief tornadoes occurred in Barry, Christian and Douglas Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn and home suffered minor damage near Highway 14 and County Road 352.

There were several reports of damaging winds and large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain developed over southern Missouri on the night of the 27th and continued through the evening of the 28th as a slow moving cold front moved across the region and interacted with a moist and unstable airmass over the region. Several inches of rain fell in a short period of time during the early morning of the 28th and led to flash flooding in Reeds Spring and at Roaring River State Park, where numerous campers were evacuated. Between 1.50 and 5 inches of rain fell across much of the Ozarks in a 24 hour period. Some readings indicated that as much as 2.5 inches of rain fell in about a 30 minute period In Barry and Stone Counties. In addition to the flooding, several brief tornadoes occurred in Barry, Christian and Douglas Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 76 was closed to traffic in both directions at a low water area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three consecutive nights of frost or freezing temperatures were observed from the 1st to the 3rd. Vegetation was vulnerable to frost and freezing temperatures due to abnormally warm temperatures in March that promoted early growth. The coldest temperatures were on the morning of the 2nd, when lows were in the 20's. The exception was the Bootheel area from Sikeston south and east, where lows were in the lower 30's. Minor to moderate crop damage was reported after a full assessment was conducted. Alfalfa and wheat sustained light damage in many areas. More than half the peach and apple crop sustained no damage, however a significant percentage of both crops were damaged to varying degrees, ranging from light to severe. Protected strawberries were undamaged, and the overall condition of the crop was rated as good in early May. An unseasonably strong high pressure system with a central pressure around 1035 mb moved southeast from Canada across the lower Ohio Valley on the morning of the 2nd.

An upper-level closed low forced a cold front move east across the region. This cold front provided the focus for showers and thunderstorms to develop and spread into the forecast area. A few of these thunderstorms became severe during the afternoon and evening hours. There were several reports of damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds tore a screen door off of a house, uprooted a large tree, and snapped off numerous tree limbs. Also, the winds caused minor damage to a machine shed and knocked down a dog pen.

A cold front moved through the region triggering showers and storms. A few of the storms produced damaging winds. One person was killed and another injured when a dead tree was blown over onto a moving car three miles west of Glover in Iron County Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down numerous large tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of unseasonably cold weather gripped central and southwest Missouri between February 7 and February 18. The coldest temperatures and wind chills occurred from February 14 through February 16. Record to near record low temperatures were common on the mornings of February 15 and 16th with subzero lows and highs just in the single digits and teens above zero. In addition, wind chill readings between -20 and -30 were reported across the area.||Along with the bitter cold, widespread snow occurred across central and southwest Missouri from the early morning hours of February 14 into the late afternoon hours of February 15. This created widespread snow covered roads and the cold and snow combined resulted in numerous if not all schools being closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the morning of February 15th, a minimum wind chill of -21 degrees was reported 3 miles north northeast of Table Rock, with -19 degrees at the Branson AWOS and -14 in Branson. In addition, the morning lows on February 16th ranged from -7 degrees in Forsyth to -14 degrees in Branson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought a round of precipitation into central and southern Missouri during the evening hours of February 7 through February 9. Intermittent freezing drizzle and freezing rain resulted in icy roads across the region, with a number of accidents. Some snow was mixed in at times, especially over central Missouri. Schools were closed February 8th and 9th due to the icy roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS unit at the Springfield/Branson National Airport reported 0.22 inches of flat ice accumulation. Roads become ice covered and hazardous with numerous accidents reported, several with injuries, and over an estimated $10,000 in indirect damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of well below normal and at times record temperatures and light snowfall events culminated in a classic winter storm on Presidents Day that brought up to 10-12 inches of snow to the county warning area. An anomalously cold Arctic air mass had engulfed the region for a week prior to this event, keeping temperatures below freezing. A renewed shot of Arctic air surged into the area over the weekend, and temperatures struggled to make it out of the single digits Sunday and Monday, with lows dropping below zero. Deep atmospheric lift provided by coupled upper-level jet streaks, an approaching mid-level trough, and mid-level frontogenesis took advantage of modest Gulf moisture moving north into the mid-Mississippi Valley to produce snow, heavy at times, Monday the 15th of February. The highest snowfall amounts occurred in east-central and southeast Missouri and southern Illinois where stout lift and the highest moisture resided.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of snow beginning the evening of February 14th. The last round of accumulating snow exited Crawford County by 6 pm on February 15th. Overall, snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of storms, followed by a bowing line of thunderstorms, organized and accelerated east-northeastward during the overnight and wee morning hours. Several pockets of damaging winds accompanied the storms. In addition, a mesovortex along the bowing line of storms produced a tornado near the Mississippi River. A mid-level shortwave trough moved east across the southern Plains. A belt of stronger southwesterly 500 mb winds spread northeast ahead of the trough into southeast Missouri and Arkansas. Instability was sufficient for a round of severe thunderstorms to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was plenty of tree damage in and near Doniphan, along with power outages. At one point, about 1700 utility customers or 25 percent of the county was without power. A large tree was down on a home in Doniphan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of unseasonably cold weather gripped central and southwest Missouri between February 7 and February 18. The coldest temperatures and wind chills occurred from February 14 through February 16. Record to near record low temperatures were common on the mornings of February 15 and 16th with subzero lows and highs just in the single digits and teens above zero. In addition, wind chill readings between -20 and -30 were reported across the area.||Along with the bitter cold, widespread snow occurred across central and southwest Missouri from the early morning hours of February 14 into the late afternoon hours of February 15. This created widespread snow covered roads and the cold and snow combined resulted in numerous if not all schools being closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A minimum wind chill of -26 degrees was reported by Mesonet station KSGF at the Springfield Regional Airport the morning of February 15th. In addition, morning low temperatures February 16th ranged from -8 degrees at the downtown Springfield airport to -18 degrees 3 miles southwest of Strafford. The Springfield-Branson Regional Airport reported a low temperature of -15 degrees.  The cold caused 18 water main breaks in the city of Springfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of storms, followed by a bowing line of thunderstorms, organized and accelerated east-northeastward during the overnight and wee morning hours. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began in open farmland, where it uprooted and snapped several large trees along Highway 77, then proceeded east-northeast toward the Mississippi River, damaging a pivot sprinkler system and totally destroying an equipment shed near the levee.  Many trees on the east side of the levee had trunks and limbs snapped, and some trees were uprooted. To the south of the tornado path, sporadic straight-line wind damage occurred to trees along Highway A (a levee road) and along County Road 507 (a levee road). Peak winds were estimated near 105 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms produced some heavy rain and small hail. One hailstone reached 1 inch in diameter.

A renewed shot of Arctic air surged into the area over the weekend, and temperatures struggled to make it out of the single digits Sunday and Monday, with lows dropping below zero. Deep atmospheric lift provided by coupled upper-level jet streaks, an approaching mid-level trough, and mid-level frontogenesis took advantage of modest Gulf moisture moving north into the mid-Mississippi Valley to produce snow, heavy at times, Monday the 15th of February. The highest snowfall amounts occurred in east-central and southeast Missouri and southern Illinois where stout lift and the highest moisture resided.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of snow beginning the evening of February 14th. The last round of accumulating snow exited Lincoln County by 8 pm on February 15th. Overall, snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 8 inches.

An extended period of unseasonably cold weather gripped central and southwest Missouri between February 7 and February 18. The coldest temperatures and wind chills occurred from February 14 through February 16. Record to near record low temperatures were common on the mornings of February 15 and 16th with subzero lows and highs just in the single digits and teens above zero. In addition, wind chill readings between -20 and -30 were reported across the area.||Along with the bitter cold, widespread snow occurred across central and southwest Missouri from the early morning hours of February 14 into the late afternoon hours of February 15. This created widespread snow covered roads and the cold and snow combined resulted in numerous if not all schools being closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A minimum wind chill of -23 degrees was reported by the Monett AWOS in Monett the morning of February 15th. In addition, morning low temperatures on February 16th ranged from -11 degrees in Cassville to -16 degrees in Wheaton.

Strong to severe storms developed during the afternoon and evening ahead of a cold front approaching from the northwest. Numerous reports of wind gusts over 60 mph and damage to trees and powerlines occurred across portions of southwest Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was uprooted and blocking the road on JJ Highway. Additional trees were blocking Highway 95 between Highway 5 and Thornfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season cold snap brought freezing temperatures down into the 20s to southern and Central Missouri on both the mornings of April 21 and April 22.  As a result of an unusually warm period in green up and bloom of area fruit trees and early emergence of specialty crops such as strawberries, grapes, blueberries, pawpaws, peaches, apples, walnuts and pecans, varying degrees of damage and bloom kill from the frost and hard freeze occurred. Row crops such as wheat, corn and soybeans saw little impacts in southwestern Missouri. No losses to livestock were reported. ||Information provided by University of Missouri Extension, USDA and FSA agents indicated that in some areas, losses of some of the specialty crops were as high as 90 percent or more, while others suffered little or not at all. Across southwestern Missouri, reports were received of losses to peaches of 75 percent or more and apples of 50 percent or greater in Webster County. In Phelps County, a vineyard reported a loss of 90 percent or more of their crop. A report from Barry County indicated that pecans and walnuts saw a nearly 50 percent reduction due to the freeze.The losses are estimates based on average acreage, production and yield and from the USDA, and commodity price data from 2011 to 2020 yield average prices for the 34 counties in southwestern Missouri covered by NWS Springfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures dipped into the middle to upper 20s both the morning of April 21 and April 22. The Cooperative Observer in Iberia reported a low of 29 degrees on April 21 and 28 degrees in April 22, Osage Beach reaching a low of 24 on April 22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and thunderstorms formed during the heat of the afternoon near a weak surface trough over the middle Mississippi Valley. The storms were on the back side of a weakening 500 mb low over the Ohio Valley. A funnel cloud was observed with one of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple eyewitnesses confirmed that a funnel cloud occurred a couple miles northwest of Baker.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed along a weak warm front that extended southeast from a low pressure center over the central Plains. Hail and gusty winds accompanied the strongest storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough moved southeast through the region, generating a complex of showers and storms.  Numerous reports of damaging winds, a few tornadoes and flash flooding were reported with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down to the northeast of Renseselaer very early in the morning on June 25th. The tornado first touched down along County Road 429. The tornado quickly pushed northeast, doing damage to a few sheds at the intersection of County Road 429 and Highway MM. One shed was completely destroyed and its roof was lifted into a nearby tree. The tornado spread northeast across Highway MM, before lifting just to the north of Highway MM. The tornado was rated EF0 with a path length of 0.21 miles and a max path width of 50 yards. No injuries or deaths were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough moved southeast through the region, generating a complex of showers and storms.  Numerous reports of damaging winds, a few tornadoes and flash flooding were reported with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief, narrow tornado touched down in a residential subdivision northwest of Hannibal, MO early Friday morning. The tornado first touched down along Rosewood Drive, where it ripped the siding off the westward facing wall of a residential home. The tornado then continued northeast towards Deer Ridge, completely destroying a couple of vinyl fences and breaking limbs off a large tree on the north side of Deer Ridge. The tornado quickly lifted as it pushed east towards Munger Lane. The tornado was rated EF0 with a path length of 0.13 miles and a max path width of 25 yards. No injuries or deaths were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season cold snap brought freezing temperatures down into the 20s to southern and Central Missouri on both the mornings of April 21 and April 22.  As a result of an unusually warm period in green up and bloom of area fruit trees and early emergence of specialty crops such as strawberries, grapes, blueberries, pawpaws, peaches, apples, walnuts and pecans, varying degrees of damage and bloom kill from the frost and hard freeze occurred. Row crops such as wheat, corn and soybeans saw little impacts in southwestern Missouri. No losses to livestock were reported. ||Information provided by University of Missouri Extension, USDA and FSA agents indicated that in some areas, losses of some of the specialty crops were as high as 90 percent or more, while others suffered little or not at all. Across southwestern Missouri, reports were received of losses to peaches of 75 percent or more and apples of 50 percent or greater in Webster County. In Phelps County, a vineyard reported a loss of 90 percent or more of their crop. A report from Barry County indicated that pecans and walnuts saw a nearly 50 percent reduction due to the freeze.The losses are estimates based on average acreage, production and yield and from the USDA, and commodity price data from 2011 to 2020 yield average prices for the 34 counties in southwestern Missouri covered by NWS Springfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread low temperatures around 30 degrees occurred the morning of April 21, while the coldest lows occurred the morning of April 22, with Camdenton reporting a low of 27, and the mesonet station 3 miles southwest of Sunrise Beach reported a low of 28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A composite outflow boundary extending from the Kansas City area towards southeast Missouri was a focus for new development from the evening hours of June 25th through June 26th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another round of heavy rain fell across Boone County, less than 12 hours after the flash flood event from June 24th through the morning hours of June 25th, where up to 7 inches of rain fell. Up to an additional 2 to 3 inches fell causing another round of flash flooding across the county. Numerous roads were flooded.  One water rescue due to swift water took place about 3 miles northeast of Murry. Another swift water rescue occurred about 2 miles northeast of Easley where two occupants of a vehicle were trapped by the flood waters.  Two vehicles were stuck in swift water from Rocky Fork Creek on North Creekland Drive, just north of intersection with East Calvert Hill Road, so the occupants had to be rescued.  Three miles northeast of McBaine, water was flooding a residence, so fire fighters had to rescue the two occupants and their dog.  A section of road was washed out about 5 miles east southeast of Harrisburg.  No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures fell below freezing both the morning of April 21 and April 22, with lows both days in the middle and upper 20s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of storms, followed by a bowing line of thunderstorms, organized and accelerated east-northeastward during the overnight and wee morning hours. Several pockets of damaging winds accompanied the storms. In addition, a mesovortex along the bowing line of storms produced a tornado near the Mississippi River. A mid-level shortwave trough moved east across the southern Plains. A belt of stronger southwesterly 500 mb winds spread northeast ahead of the trough into southeast Missouri and Arkansas. Instability was sufficient for a round of severe thunderstorms to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over the roadway at County Road 659 and Highway ZZ southeast of Dudley. Swift water was flowing across Highway WW south of County Road 420. A few other roads in the county were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary from earlier convection moved southward through region, triggering widespread storms. Some of the storms became severe with numerous reports of large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down a 31 foot by 70 foot pole barn, with the debris blown south across the roadway two miles west northwest of Thompson. Also, several power lines were blown down between Thompson and the Audrain/Callaway County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system deepened rapidly as it moved from southwest Kansas to southwest Missouri to southern Illinois. Strong to severe storms that developed over northern Oklahoma and central Kansas around midnight on the 17th pushed eastward into southeast Kansas and western Missouri by daybreak. Several of these storms produced hail to the size of quarters and wind gusts over 60 mph during the early morning. ||After the morning storms dissipated, with the loss of support from the nocturnal low level jet,  residual outflow boundaries and increasing lift from the approaching upper level low led to another round of intense storms that developed over northwest Arkansas and northeast Oklahoma during the mid afternoon and moved northeastward across southwest Missouri through the evening. These storms produced a few tornadoes over Barry and Stone Counties and hail up to 2 inches in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept through the region on March 27th. The low level forcing along the front, increasing upper level ascent downstream of an approaching shortwave, and at least modest moisture and instability were sufficient to generate thunderstorms as the front moved through the region. There were numerous reports of large hail and damaging winds as well as one tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several large tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm tracked east-southeastward across Perry and northern Bollinger Counties. This storm was part of a larger complex of showers and storms that dumped locally heavy rain. A deep upper-level low pressure system sank southeast into the far southwestern U.S., pumping plenty  of Pacific moisture northeast into the mid Mississippi River Valley as a cold front settled slowly southeast into the region. There was just enough instability for scattered thunderstorms, including the isolated severe storm. The heavy rain caused minor river flooding on the St. Francis River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down across the southbound lane of Highway 51.

On the morning of March 17, 2021 elevated thunderstorms pushed northward, causing isolated severe hail.

March brought a return to warmer weather with an active spring-like pattern. There were a couple rounds of heavier rain, somewhere within the Mississippi basin, regionally that contributed to additional river flooding. Monthly precipitation was above normal at most locations, as much as 150 to 200 percent above normal in the lower Missouri and mid Mississippi Valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along the Mississippi River during the latter half of the month. The river crested at 37.83 feet on the 22nd, which is several feet above the 32-foot flood stage. This required the closure of a couple flood gates in the Cape Girardeau flood wall. Floodwaters backed up a few tributary creeks and small rivers, causing considerable flooding of fields well away from the mainstem river. The river remained above flood stage going into April.

There were a couple rounds of heavier rain, somewhere within the Mississippi basin, regionally that contributed to additional river flooding. Monthly precipitation was above normal at most locations, as much as 150 to 200 percent above normal in the lower Missouri and mid Mississippi Valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor to moderate flooding of the Mississippi River caused considerable flooding of fields and farmland.

Several of these storms produced hail to the size of quarters and wind gusts over 60 mph during the early morning. ||After the morning storms dissipated, with the loss of support from the nocturnal low level jet,  residual outflow boundaries and increasing lift from the approaching upper level low led to another round of intense storms that developed over northwest Arkansas and northeast Oklahoma during the mid afternoon and moved northeastward across southwest Missouri through the evening. These storms produced a few tornadoes over Barry and Stone Counties and hail up to 2 inches in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway B in Wheaton was closed due to flooding of a low water area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region triggering showers and storms.  Some of the storms became severe with several reports of damaging winds and large hail.
A cold front moved through the region triggering showers and storms.  Some of the storms became severe with several reports of damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds snapped off several trees that were 10 to 12 inches in diameter on the south side of Fredericktown. One of the trees fell against a house causing moderate damage. A roof was torn off of a metal garage as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated convection developed in the early morning hours over portions of central Missouri. Some of the storms became severe with isolated reports of large hail.
Elevated convection developed in the early morning hours over portions of central Missouri. Some of the storms became severe with isolated reports of large hail.

An outflow boundary from earlier convection moved southward through region, triggering widespread storms. Some of the storms became severe with numerous reports of large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wide swath of damaging winds was reported across the western portions of St. Louis County. A 77 mph wind gust was recorded at Spirit of St. Louis Airport at 528 pm. Numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines were blown down in Chesterfield, Wildwood, Ellisville, Clarkson Valley and Ballwin. A large tree was uprooted onto a house in Chesterfield causing moderate roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm that produced a weak tornado in eastern Johnson County Kansas moved into western Missouri and an eyewitness reported a brief touchdown near 203rd and HWY 291. No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm that produced a weak tornado in eastern Johnson County Kansas moved into western Missouri and an eyewitness reported a brief touchdown near 203rd and HWY 291. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept through the region on March 27th. The low level forcing along the front, increasing upper level ascent downstream of an approaching shortwave, and at least modest moisture and instability were sufficient to generate thunderstorms as the front moved through the region. There were numerous reports of large hail and damaging winds as well as one tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wide swath of large hail fell across the southern portions of St. Charles County. The National Weather Service reported golf ball sized hail at the office in Weldon Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread rain with embedded thunderstorms trained over the region as moisture overran a warm front along the Arkansas border. Accumulations over two inches occurred over central and southwest Missouri, with isolated accumulations of three inches in Douglas County. Numerous low water crossings were flooded leading to road closures along state routes. One water rescue occurred on Highway YY near Norwood in Douglas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused sections of Greenwood and Cologne Roads to be closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain spread across the region during the late evening of the 29th and continued into the evening of the 30th as a low pressure system moved from the Texas Panhandle to southern Indiana. Widespread rainfall accumulations of one to two inches on already saturated soils led to another round of flooding on some of the same locations that flooded on the 25th. During the afternoon, a few strong to severe storms produced hail to the size of quarters and isolated wind damage over Barry, Lawrence and McDonald counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused the North Fork River to flood a section of Highway EE making it impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the overnight hours on New Years Eve, into the morning of New Years moderate freezing rain moved into southern, western, and central Missouri. While rain was a little heavier than traditionally efficient for ice accumulation, there was a quick quarter to three quarters inch of ice that accumulated on surfaces before light to moderate snow moved in for the daytime hours. This snow accumulated a few inches on top of the ice accretion. Overall minimal impacts were felt as a result of this system aside from scattered vehicle accidents and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate freezing rain formed early morning on New Year's Day. This freezing rain quickly accreted between around a quarter inch before sunrise. Shortly after sunrise the freezing rain switched to light snow, which persisted for the bulk of the remaining day time hours. The snow did not accumulate more than 1 to 3 inches, but on top of the ice accretion created winter storm conditions. Mostly scattered power outages and vehicle accidents were the impacts, but being a holiday, road traffic was likely light.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system lifted northward through Arkansas and into Missouri from New Years Eve into New Years Day. Freezing rain spread into southeast Kansas and southern and central Missouri during the evening hours of Thursday, December 31. The freezing rain continued into January 1, 2021 before transitioning over to minor accumulations of snow. Ice accumulations overnight and into January 1, 2021 resulted in tree damage and scattered power outages. Once the freezing rain changes to snow with a dusting to 1.5 inches of accumulation was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a continuation of the storm from December 31, 2020. The cooperative observer 4 miles east southeast of Flemington reported 0.25 inches of radial ice accretion measured on tree limbs. This converts to 0.63 inches of flat ice accretion. Elsewhere, a public report via social media showed a measurement of one eighth of an inch of radial ice accretion in Aldrich, which converts to 0.32 inches of flat ice accretion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low occluded prior to it reaching the forecast area with a new surface low developing and tracking further east. This lead to cooler surface temperatures and a cooler warm nose aloft, which impacted precipitation accumulations and types for portions of the forecast area. Wintry mix at the onset of precipitation changed over to freezing rain. The impacts of the cooler surface temperatures kept the precipitation type as freezing rain a bit longer and this lead to higher ice accumulation amounts across the forecast area. This was particularly notable in portions of central and northeastern Missouri, where up to four tenths of an inch of icing occurred. As the system exited region, the precipitation transitioned to some sleet and snow before ending.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread freezing rain moved into Lewis County after 4 am and persisted through 8 am, then a bit of a break before light snow developed and moved in around 3 pm. Overall a quarter of an inch of ice accumulation with up to seven tenths of an inch of sleet on top.

This lead to cooler surface temperatures and a cooler warm nose aloft, which impacted precipitation accumulations and types for portions of the forecast area. Wintry mix at the onset of precipitation changed over to freezing rain. The impacts of the cooler surface temperatures kept the precipitation type as freezing rain a bit longer and this lead to higher ice accumulation amounts across the forecast area. This was particularly notable in portions of central and northeastern Missouri, where up to four tenths of an inch of icing occurred. As the system exited region, the precipitation transitioned to some sleet and snow before ending.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread freezing rain moved into Osage County after midnight and persisted through 530 am, then a bit of a break before light snow developed and moved in around Noon. Overall three tenths of an inch of ice accumulation with up to half an inch of snow on top.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread rain with embedded thunderstorms trained over the region as moisture overran a warm front along the Arkansas border. Accumulations over two inches occurred over central and southwest Missouri, with isolated accumulations of three inches in Douglas County. Numerous low water crossings were flooded leading to road closures along state routes. One water rescue occurred on Highway YY near Norwood in Douglas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused the Niangua River to flood making Highway Y impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain spread across the region during the late evening of the 29th and continued into the evening of the 30th as a low pressure system moved from the Texas Panhandle to southern Indiana. Widespread rainfall accumulations of one to two inches on already saturated soils led to another round of flooding on some of the same locations that flooded on the 25th. During the afternoon, a few strong to severe storms produced hail to the size of quarters and isolated wind damage over Barry, Lawrence and McDonald counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused Cowskin Creek to flood a section of Highway Y making it impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the overnight hours on New Years Eve, into the morning of New Years moderate freezing rain moved into southern, western, and central Missouri. While rain was a little heavier than traditionally efficient for ice accumulation, there was a quick quarter to three quarters inch of ice that accumulated on surfaces before light to moderate snow moved in for the daytime hours. This snow accumulated a few inches on top of the ice accretion. Overall minimal impacts were felt as a result of this system aside from scattered vehicle accidents and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate freezing rain formed early morning on New Year's Day. This freezing rain quickly accreted between a quarter to three quarters inch before sunrise. Shortly after sunrise the freezing rain switched to light snow, which persisted for the bulk of the remaining day time hours. The snow did not accumulate more than 1 to 3 inches, but on top of the ice accretion created winter storm conditions. Mostly scattered power outages and vehicle accidents were the impacts, but being a holiday, road traffic was likely light.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system lifted northward through Arkansas and into Missouri from New Years Eve into New Years Day. Freezing rain spread into southeast Kansas and southern and central Missouri during the evening hours of Thursday, December 31. The freezing rain continued into January 1, 2021 before transitioning over to minor accumulations of snow. Ice accumulations overnight and into January 1, 2021 resulted in tree damage and scattered power outages. Once the freezing rain changes to snow with a dusting to 1.5 inches of accumulation was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of the storm from December 31, 2020. Emergency Manager reported a flat ice accumulation of 0.25 inches on elevated objects in the city of Buffalo. Elsewhere, widespread flat ice accumulations between 0.25 and 0.50 inches were reported via public across the county. Utility companies reported power outages due to ice coated limbs falling on power lines.

The cooperative observer 2 miles north northeast of Northview reported radial ice accretion on tree limbs between 0.20 and 0.25 inches. This converts to between 0.50 and 0.63 inches of flat ice accretion. Some power outages were reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow began in southeast Missouri during the early to mid morning hours. Highest amounts were focused across the Ozark Foothills of southeast Missouri, aided by the earlier start time of the snow and colder air and ground temperatures. Total accumulations were around an inch for areas near the Kentucky border in Mississippi County. Amounts ranged upward to 3 to 4 inches in the hills north and west of Poplar Bluff. Specific amounts included 3.5 inches in Poplar Bluff, 1.5 inches near the Cape Girardeau airport, and one inch at Perryville. Roads were snow-covered and slippery. The snow fell at the rate of 1 to 1.5 inches an hour at Dudley in Stoddard County. A surface low occluded prior to it reaching the forecast area with a new surface low developing and tracking further east. This lead to cooler surface temperatures and a cooler warm nose aloft, which impacted precipitation accumulations and types for portions of the forecast area. Wintry mix at the onset of precipitation changed over to freezing rain. The impacts of the cooler surface temperatures kept the precipitation type as freezing rain a bit longer and this lead to higher ice accumulation amounts across the forecast area. This was particularly notable in portions of central and northeastern Missouri, where up to four tenths of an inch of icing occurred. As the system exited region, the precipitation transitioned to some sleet and snow before ending.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread freezing rain moved into Monroe County after 130 am and persisted through 7 am, then a bit of a break before light snow developed and moved in around 2 pm. Overall between a quarter to half of an inch of ice accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Within an area of scattered convection an isolated weak tornado formed and damaged power lines and power poles within a narrow south to north path on two west-east roads about a mile apart.

During the overnight hours on New Years Eve, into the morning of New Years moderate freezing rain moved into southern, western, and central Missouri. While rain was a little heavier than traditionally efficient for ice accumulation, there was a quick quarter to three quarters inch of ice that accumulated on surfaces before light to moderate snow moved in for the daytime hours. This snow accumulated a few inches on top of the ice accretion. Overall minimal impacts were felt as a result of this system aside from scattered vehicle accidents and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate freezing rain formed early morning on New Year's Day. This freezing rain quickly accreted between a quarter to half inch before sunrise. Shortly after sunrise the freezing rain switched to light snow, which persisted for the bulk of the remaining day time hours. The snow did not accumulate more than 1 to 3 inches, but on top of the ice accretion created winter storm conditions. Mostly scattered power outages and vehicle accidents were the impacts, but being a holiday, road traffic was likely light.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of January 25, 2021 light to moderate snow moved into northern Missouri. Widespread amounts were around 6 inches, but some areas across far northern Missouri reached the 8 to 12 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell through the day on January 25. While the heavier snow fell across far northern Missouri into southern and central Iowa, there was enough snow in Adair County to cause a vehicle accident that resulted in injuries and at least two fatalities. In the middle of the event, around 2 pm LST, the head-on vehicle collision occurred when one vehicle lost control on an icy or snow covered roadway and veered into oncoming traffic. ||Missouri Highway Patrol Crash Report:  https://www.mshp.dps.missouri.gov/HP68/AccidentDetailsAction?ACC_RPT_NUM=210040140 .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system lifted northward through Arkansas and into Missouri from New Years Eve into New Years Day. Freezing rain spread into southeast Kansas and southern and central Missouri during the evening hours of Thursday, December 31. The freezing rain continued into January 1, 2021 before transitioning over to minor accumulations of snow. Ice accumulations overnight and into January 1, 2021 resulted in tree damage and scattered power outages. Once the freezing rain changes to snow with a dusting to 1.5 inches of accumulation was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a continuation of the storm from December 31, 2020. Law enforcement in Neosho reported 0.15 inches of flat ice accumulation. The freezing rain changed to snow by late morning with a total of 2.5 inches of snowfall reported in Neosho.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system lifted northward through Arkansas and into Missouri from New Years Eve into New Years Day. Freezing rain spread into southeast Kansas and southern and central Missouri during the evening hours of Thursday, December 31. The freezing rain continued into January 1, 2021 before transitioning over to minor accumulations of snow. Ice accumulations overnight and into January 1, 2021 resulted in tree damage and scattered power outages. Once the freezing rain changes to snow with a dusting to 1.5 inches of accumulation was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a continuation of the December 31, 2020 storm. Estimates of 0.20 inches of flat ice accumulation across the norther portion of the county. The freezing rain did change to snow with up to an inch of accumulation ,mainly across the northern portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow began in southeast Missouri during the early to mid morning hours. Highest amounts were focused across the Ozark Foothills of southeast Missouri, aided by the earlier start time of the snow and colder air and ground temperatures. Total accumulations were around an inch for areas near the Kentucky border in Mississippi County. Amounts ranged upward to 3 to 4 inches in the hills north and west of Poplar Bluff. Specific amounts included 3.5 inches in Poplar Bluff, 1.5 inches near the Cape Girardeau airport, and one inch at Perryville. Roads were snow-covered and slippery. The snow fell at the rate of 1 to 1.5 inches an hour at Dudley in Stoddard County. The snow was caused by a low pressure system that tracked northeast from the central Gulf coast to the northeast Georgia mountains.

A weaker winter storm moved into the area and added more ice and snow to the snow pack received the other day. This system moved in from the south and brought along a strong pull of warm air aloft. This led to a lot more ice accumulation than was expected prior to the event. Mainly freezing rain and sleet fell through most of the afternoon. This led to widespread ice accumulations of 0.10-0.25. Snow and sleet accumulations across northeast Missouri ranged from 2 to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter near Charlie Heath Memorial Conservation Area reported 4.0 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light accumulations of ice glazed elevated surfaces, such as trees and power lines. Icing of up to one-tenth inch was measured on flat surfaces. There was little impact on travel. The ice was mainly from the Cape Girardeau area north and west. The precipitation occurred in association with a deep 500 mb and surface low that moved north-northeast across Texas into the Plains.

A few hours of heavy wet snow caused slippery road conditions in the Ozark foothills of southeast Missouri. Roads were snow-covered and slippery during the height of the storm, when visibility was reduced to one-half mile or less. One to three inches of snow was common, including a report of 3 inches at Ellsinore in Carter County. The snow occurred on the northern fringe of the precipitation shield associated with a low pressure center that tracked east through the central Gulf Coast states.

An upper-level disturbance moved into the area as cold air arrived at the surface. As a result, rain quickly changed over to snow and accumulated about an inch across northern parts of Dunklin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About an inch of snow fell over northern Dunklin County, mainly on elevated surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rain resulted in some isolated flash flooding in the Ozark foothills. As a  500 mb trough ejected into the central U.S, a prefrontal surface trough acted to quickly spread a swath of heavy rain into the region by daybreak. A surface warm front approached the region from the south, adding to rainfall totals during the day. Within a large area of steady rain, isolated elevated thunderstorms occurred north of the warm front. Rainfall over an inch per hour was reported in the heaviest downpours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low water crossings were full. Water was over a couple of streets in Van Buren.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of January 25, 2021 light to moderate snow moved into northern Missouri. Widespread amounts were around 6 inches, but some areas across far northern Missouri reached the 8 to 12 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow moved into far northwest Missouri on the morning of January 25, by mid day roughly 6 inches of snow fell, and by the end of the event roughly 6 to 7 inches of snow fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the overnight hours on New Years Eve, into the morning of New Years moderate freezing rain moved into southern, western, and central Missouri. While rain was a little heavier than traditionally efficient for ice accumulation, there was a quick quarter to three quarters inch of ice that accumulated on surfaces before light to moderate snow moved in for the daytime hours. This snow accumulated a few inches on top of the ice accretion. Overall minimal impacts were felt as a result of this system aside from scattered vehicle accidents and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate freezing rain formed early morning on New Year's Day. This freezing rain quickly accreted between a quarter to three quarters inch before sunrise. Shortly after sunrise the freezing rain switched to light snow, which persisted for the bulk of the remaining day time hours. The snow did not accumulate more than 1 to 3 inches, but on top of the ice accretion created winter storm conditions. Mostly scattered power outages and vehicle accidents were the impacts, but being a holiday, road traffic was likely light.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread rain with embedded thunderstorms trained over the region as moisture overran a warm front along the Arkansas border. Accumulations over two inches occurred over central and southwest Missouri, with isolated accumulations of three inches in Douglas County. Numerous low water crossings were flooded leading to road closures along state routes. One water rescue occurred on Highway YY near Norwood in Douglas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused Bryant Creek to flood making Missouri Route 181 impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain spread across the region during the late evening of the 29th and continued into the evening of the 30th as a low pressure system moved from the Texas Panhandle to southern Indiana. Widespread rainfall accumulations of one to two inches on already saturated soils led to another round of flooding on some of the same locations that flooded on the 25th. During the afternoon, a few strong to severe storms produced hail to the size of quarters and isolated wind damage over Barry, Lawrence and McDonald counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds caused minor damage to a few barns and knocked over several large trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low occluded prior to it reaching the forecast area with a new surface low developing and tracking further east. This lead to cooler surface temperatures and a cooler warm nose aloft, which impacted precipitation accumulations and types for portions of the forecast area. Wintry mix at the onset of precipitation changed over to freezing rain. The impacts of the cooler surface temperatures kept the precipitation type as freezing rain a bit longer and this lead to higher ice accumulation amounts across the forecast area. This was particularly notable in portions of central and northeastern Missouri, where up to four tenths of an inch of icing occurred. As the system exited region, the precipitation transitioned to some sleet and snow before ending.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread freezing rain moved into Boone County after midnight and persisted through 630 am, then a bit of a break before light snow developed and moved in around 1 pm. Overall around three tenths of an inch of ice accumulation with up to half an inch of snow on top. There were several reports of power lines knocked down as well as several trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 5, a cold air mass settled into the center part of the country, which caused temperatures to go below freezing. They would not return to the freezing/melting point until several weeks later. The worst of the cold snap occurred between February 14 through February 16. Aside from the cold air arrival, several inches of snow fell, which allowed the already historically cold airmass to hold together. Minimum temperatures on Monday the 15th and and Tuesday the 16th reached negative double digits, and daytime highs across the area failed to reach zero degrees. In Kansas City, this marked the first time for a negative high temperature since a previous historic cold air outbreak in December of 1989. Even more rare was the 10-20 mph wind that accompanied the cold airmass, which caused wind chills each day to approach the 20 to 30 degree below zero mark. ||Some benchmarks that were hit during this cold outbreak: New daily record low temperatures were established at Kansas City on February 15 and 16. On February 15 the low at Kansas City International Airport - the official measuring site - dropped to -10 degrees, which broke the previous record of -6 degrees from 1936. On February 15, Kansas City did not warm up to zero, making it the first sub-zero high temperature since December 22, 1989. On February 16 the overnight low dropped even lower, recording a temperature of -13 degrees. This broke the daily low of -8 degrees, set in 1979.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the first night of bitter cold across the area, temperatures dropped well below zero and with winds around 10-20 mph wind chills overnight going into Sunday morning dropped to around 20 to 30 below.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of precipitation impacted the region resulting in icy roads, numerous accidents, and road closures. The event started in the early morning hours of February 10th and continued into the night of the 10th. Numerous accidents occurred from the morning hours of the 10th into the morning of the 11th. This resulted in lengthy road closures on sections of I-55 between Cape Girardeau and Portageville, MO, and portions of I-57 from Sikeston, MO to Mounds, IL. Ice accumulations ranging from 0.1 to 0.25 were commonplace across areas along and east of I-55 in southeast Missouri. Most areas received at least minor accumulations of sleet,  but the heaviest swath of sleet and snow amounts ranging from 0.5 to 1.5 occurred north and west of Cape Girardeau, including the Poplar Bluff area. The winter storm was the result of a moist southwest 500 mb flow and an embedded low-amplitude impulse. An inverted surface trough developing in the general vicinity of Arkansas brought another swath of precipitation. As the inverted trough pulled away to the east, colder air was drawn southward and changed the freezing rain to sleet.

An initial round of light snow impacted southeast Missouri during the early morning hours on the 17th. After a lull, snow spread northeastward from Arkansas during the late afternoon and early evening hours. The most concentrated area of snow occurred near the Missouri Bootheel during the evening and overnight hours. The last snow band pivoted across the region during the morning of the 18th. Snowfall totals ranged from 1 to 3 for this event. The highest totals were primarily near the Bootheel, from New Madrid to southern Butler County. With temperatures well below freezing, snow quickly accumulated on roads and created treacherous travel conditions from the evening of the 17th through the morning of the 18th. Following on the heels of a major winter storm on the 15th, a mid level short wave shifted eastward into the Plains as surface low pressure lifted east northeast out of the northern Gulf and into the southeastern part of the country. Plenty of cold air remained in place for the precipitation type to be all snow with this system.

Arctic high pressure centered over the northern Plains brought dangerously low wind chills during the early and mid morning hours. Gusty north winds were accompanied by actual temperatures in the single digits and teens. Wind chills dropped as low as minus 10 to minus 15 degrees over much of southeast Missouri.

Plenty of cold air remained in place for the precipitation type to be all snow with this system.
As a cold front moved through the region with plenty of low level moisture and instability, several strong to severe storms developed. There were numerous reports of large hail and damaging winds.

On February 5, a cold air mass settled into the center part of the country, which caused temperatures to go below freezing. They would not return to the freezing/melting point until several weeks later. The worst of the cold snap occurred between February 14 through February 16. Aside from the cold air arrival, several inches of snow fell, which allowed the already historically cold airmass to hold together. Minimum temperatures on Monday the 15th and and Tuesday the 16th reached negative double digits, and daytime highs across the area failed to reach zero degrees. In Kansas City, this marked the first time for a negative high temperature since a previous historic cold air outbreak in December of 1989. Even more rare was the 10-20 mph wind that accompanied the cold airmass, which caused wind chills each day to approach the 20 to 30 degree below zero mark. ||Some benchmarks that were hit during this cold outbreak: New daily record low temperatures were established at Kansas City on February 15 and 16. On February 15 the low at Kansas City International Airport - the official measuring site - dropped to -10 degrees, which broke the previous record of -6 degrees from 1936. On February 15, Kansas City did not warm up to zero, making it the first sub-zero high temperature since December 22, 1989. On February 16 the overnight low dropped even lower, recording a temperature of -13 degrees. This broke the daily low of -8 degrees, set in 1979.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the first night of bitter cold across the area, temperatures dropped well below zero and with winds around 10-20 mph wind chills overnight going into Sunday morning dropped to around 20 to 30 below.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of precipitation impacted the region resulting in icy roads, numerous accidents, and road closures. The event started in the early morning hours of February 10th and continued into the night of the 10th. Numerous accidents occurred from the morning hours of the 10th into the morning of the 11th. This resulted in lengthy road closures on sections of I-55 between Cape Girardeau and Portageville, MO, and portions of I-57 from Sikeston, MO to Mounds, IL. Ice accumulations ranging from 0.1 to 0.25 were commonplace across areas along and east of I-55 in southeast Missouri. Most areas received at least minor accumulations of sleet,  but the heaviest swath of sleet and snow amounts ranging from 0.5 to 1.5 occurred north and west of Cape Girardeau, including the Poplar Bluff area. The winter storm was the result of a moist southwest 500 mb flow and an embedded low-amplitude impulse. An inverted surface trough developing in the general vicinity of Arkansas brought another swath of precipitation. As the inverted trough pulled away to the east, colder air was drawn southward and changed the freezing rain to sleet.

Arctic high pressure centered over the northern Plains brought dangerously low wind chills during the early and mid morning hours. Gusty north winds were accompanied by actual temperatures in the single digits and teens. Wind chills dropped as low as minus 10 to minus 15 degrees over much of southeast Missouri.

An extended period of unseasonably cold weather gripped central and southwest Missouri between February 7 and February 18. The coldest temperatures and wind chills occurred from February 14 through February 16. Record to near record low temperatures were common on the mornings of February 15 and 16th with subzero lows and highs just in the single digits and teens above zero. In addition, wind chill readings between -20 and -30 were reported across the area.||Along with the bitter cold, widespread snow occurred across central and southwest Missouri from the early morning hours of February 14 into the late afternoon hours of February 15. This created widespread snow covered roads and the cold and snow combined resulted in numerous if not all schools being closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A minimum wind chill of -26 degrees was reported at Mesonet station BLVRB at Bolivar Elementary School in Bolivar the morning of February 15th. Mesonet station FW8323 2 miles west of Bolivar reported a minimum wind chill of -25 degrees the morning of February 15th. In addition, a morning low of -18 degrees was reported by the COOP observer in Bolivar the morning of February 16th.

In addition, wind chill readings between -20 and -30 were reported across the area.||Along with the bitter cold, widespread snow occurred across central and southwest Missouri from the early morning hours of February 14 into the late afternoon hours of February 15. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report via Social Media of an estimated 4.6 inches of total snowfall 1 mile south southwest of Decaturville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of unseasonably cold weather gripped central and southwest Missouri between February 7 and February 18. The coldest temperatures and wind chills occurred from February 14 through February 16. Record to near record low temperatures were common on the mornings of February 15 and 16th with subzero lows and highs just in the single digits and teens above zero. In addition, wind chill readings between -20 and -30 were reported across the area.||Along with the bitter cold, widespread snow occurred across central and southwest Missouri from the early morning hours of February 14 into the late afternoon hours of February 15. This created widespread snow covered roads and the cold and snow combined resulted in numerous if not all schools being closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cooperative Observer 4 miles west southwest of Winona measured a storm total snowfall of 5.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 5, a cold air mass settled into the center part of the country, which caused temperatures to go below freezing. They would not return to the freezing/melting point until several weeks later. The worst of the cold snap occurred between February 14 through February 16. Aside from the cold air arrival, several inches of snow fell, which allowed the already historically cold airmass to hold together. Minimum temperatures on Monday the 15th and and Tuesday the 16th reached negative double digits, and daytime highs across the area failed to reach zero degrees. In Kansas City, this marked the first time for a negative high temperature since a previous historic cold air outbreak in December of 1989. Even more rare was the 10-20 mph wind that accompanied the cold airmass, which caused wind chills each day to approach the 20 to 30 degree below zero mark. ||Some benchmarks that were hit during this cold outbreak: New daily record low temperatures were established at Kansas City on February 15 and 16. On February 15 the low at Kansas City International Airport - the official measuring site - dropped to -10 degrees, which broke the previous record of -6 degrees from 1936. On February 15, Kansas City did not warm up to zero, making it the first sub-zero high temperature since December 22, 1989. On February 16 the overnight low dropped even lower, recording a temperature of -13 degrees. This broke the daily low of -8 degrees, set in 1979.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the first night of bitter cold across the area, temperatures dropped well below zero and with winds around 10-20 mph wind chills overnight going into Sunday morning dropped to around 20 to 30 below.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An initial round of light snow impacted southeast Missouri during the early morning hours on the 17th. After a lull, snow spread northeastward from Arkansas during the late afternoon and early evening hours. The most concentrated area of snow occurred near the Missouri Bootheel during the evening and overnight hours. The last snow band pivoted across the region during the morning of the 18th. Snowfall totals ranged from 1 to 3 for this event. The highest totals were primarily near the Bootheel, from New Madrid to southern Butler County. With temperatures well below freezing, snow quickly accumulated on roads and created treacherous travel conditions from the evening of the 17th through the morning of the 18th. Following on the heels of a major winter storm on the 15th, a mid level short wave shifted eastward into the Plains as surface low pressure lifted east northeast out of the northern Gulf and into the southeastern part of the country. Plenty of cold air remained in place for the precipitation type to be all snow with this system.

A strong storm system in the upper levels of the atmosphere moved east-northeast across the lower and mid Mississippi Valley on President's Day. A round of light snow the night before President's Day produced an inch or two in many locations. The primary period of heavy snow was on President's Day, when snowfall rates averaged one to two inches per hour. Visibility was one-quarter mile or less in the heavy snow. Snowfall totals for the winter storm varied greatly. Sleet brought snowfall totals downward across areas near the Bootheel, such as New Madrid. Due to extensive blowing and drifting, accurate snowfall amounts were difficult to obtain. Average snow and sleet amounts were lowest around New Madrid, typically about four inches. Amounts increased to the northwest, averaging from 9 to 12 inches in the hilly terrain from the Perryville area to Marble Hill, Greenville, and Van Buren. Impacts were rather high. Most schools and businesses were shut down either due to the storm or the federal holiday. Wind chills were dangerously cold, at times ranging from minus 10 to minus 15. Sub-zero wind chills were observed as far south as Tennessee. The very cold temperatures hampered removal of the snow.

In Kansas City, this marked the first time for a negative high temperature since a previous historic cold air outbreak in December of 1989. Even more rare was the 10-20 mph wind that accompanied the cold airmass, which caused wind chills each day to approach the 20 to 30 degree below zero mark. ||Some benchmarks that were hit during this cold outbreak: New daily record low temperatures were established at Kansas City on February 15 and 16. On February 15 the low at Kansas City International Airport - the official measuring site - dropped to -10 degrees, which broke the previous record of -6 degrees from 1936. On February 15, Kansas City did not warm up to zero, making it the first sub-zero high temperature since December 22, 1989. On February 16 the overnight low dropped even lower, recording a temperature of -13 degrees. This broke the daily low of -8 degrees, set in 1979.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the first night of bitter cold across the area, temperatures dropped well below zero and with winds around 10-20 mph wind chills overnight going into Sunday morning dropped to around 20 to 30 below.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An initial round of light snow impacted southeast Missouri during the early morning hours on the 17th. After a lull, snow spread northeastward from Arkansas during the late afternoon and early evening hours. The most concentrated area of snow occurred near the Missouri Bootheel during the evening and overnight hours. The last snow band pivoted across the region during the morning of the 18th. Snowfall totals ranged from 1 to 3 for this event. The highest totals were primarily near the Bootheel, from New Madrid to southern Butler County. With temperatures well below freezing, snow quickly accumulated on roads and created treacherous travel conditions from the evening of the 17th through the morning of the 18th. Following on the heels of a major winter storm on the 15th, a mid level short wave shifted eastward into the Plains as surface low pressure lifted east northeast out of the northern Gulf and into the southeastern part of the country. Plenty of cold air remained in place for the precipitation type to be all snow with this system.
